name: Procrustes
shortDescription: A rogue smith who forced travelers to fit his bed, stretching or cutting them to size until defeated by Theseus.
description: Procrustes, whose real name was Damastes or Polypemon, was a rogue smith and bandit in Attica. He operated near Eleusis, where he owned a peculiar estate. Procrustes had two beds of different sizes. He would invite travelers into his home, offering them hospitality. However, he had a sinister motive. Depending on the traveler's height, he would force them onto one of his beds. If the traveler was too short for the bed, Procrustes would hammer or stretch them to fit. If they were too long, he would amputate the excess portion of their bodies. Theseus, during his journey from Troezen to Athens, encountered Procrustes and subjected him to his own brutal methods, ultimately killing him. This act was part of Theseus's heroic cleansing of the road to Athens, ridding it of various monsters and criminals.
subtitle: The stretcher: hospitality with a deadly twist.
achievements: Maintained a deceptive façade of hospitality
Developed a gruesome method of torture and murder
Created beds of different sizes to accommodate his victims
Established a reign of fear along the road to Eleusis
Became a symbol of inflexible conformity

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: Procrustes' parentage is not consistently stated in all accounts, but he is generally depicted as a local figure of Attica. His true name was said to be Damastes or Polypemon, with 'Procrustes' being a nickname meaning 'the stretcher' or 'one who hammers out'. His myth is primarily associated with the early exploits of Theseus, who encountered him as one of the dangers during his journey to claim his birthright in Athens. The story is a cautionary tale about the dangers of forced conformity and the abuse of power. The primary source detailing the encounter is Pseudo-Apollodorus' *Bibliotheca*.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Procrustes does not hold a religious role in the Greek pantheon. Instead, he functions as a monstrous figure in the heroic narratives. His significance lies in his embodiment of injustice and cruelty. He represents the dangers of imposing rigid standards on individuals, highlighting the importance of accepting differences. Procrustes' actions serve as a stark contrast to the ideals of hospitality (xenia) that were highly valued in ancient Greek culture. His story reinforces the importance of justice and the consequences of violating ethical norms.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: The bed of Procrustes is a potent symbol of forced conformity and the suppression of individuality. It represents the idea that individuals are often pressured to fit into predefined molds, regardless of their unique characteristics or needs. The act of stretching or cutting victims to fit the bed symbolizes the violence and injustice inherent in such attempts at forced conformity. In a broader sense, Procrustes' actions can be interpreted as a critique of social norms and power structures that seek to control and homogenize individuals. The myth encourages a celebration of diversity and a rejection of rigid, inflexible standards.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: The term 'Procrustean bed' or 'Procrustean solution' has entered common usage to describe situations where an arbitrary standard is used to force things into a particular mold, often with negative consequences. The myth continues to be relevant in discussions about education, social justice, and the dangers of imposing rigid systems on complex realities. The story of Procrustes is sometimes referenced in modern literature, film, and other forms of media as a cautionary tale about the abuse of power and the importance of respecting individual differences. He remains a symbol of the dangers of inflexibility and the need for adaptable solutions.

sources.author: Pseudo-Apollodorus
sources.description: A comprehensive source of Greek mythology, containing the account of Theseus and Procrustes.

sources.author: Diodorus Siculus
sources.description: Another historical source that mentions the deeds of Theseus, including his defeat of Procrustes.

associations.name: Inflexibility
associations.significance: Procrustes embodies the dangers of rigid adherence to standards.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Raktabija
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A demon who multiplies from every drop of blood spilled, representing an unyielding, impossible-to-defeat force.

companions.name: Theseus
companions.description: The Athenian hero who defeated Procrustes as part of his labors.

cultural.modernLegacy: The term 'Procrustean bed' is used to describe situations where things are forced to fit an arbitrary standard.
